title: Dabeli | Origin
lines: Dabeli, meaning pressed, originated in Mandvi, Kutch (Gujarat) in the 1960s, invented by Keshavji Gabha Chudasama
(Kesha Malam) as an affordable, flavorful snack for the working class, blending spiced mashed potatoes with chutneys
and toppings inside a Pav. It's journey from a local Kutchi, innovation to an Indian burger, reflects its simple yet
rich flavors, becoming a staple of Indian street food culture.
Initially sold for just one anna (about six paise), After the division of Bombay State, Gujarati migrants and vendors
carried Dabeli to Mumbai, where it became a staple. It quickly became a beloved street food, spreading from Gujarat
to Maharashtra and, beyond, known for its unique mix, of sweet spicy, tangy flavors and textures.

title: Chicken 65 | Origin
lines: Chicken 65 originated in Chennai at the legendary Buhari Hotel in 1965, created by A.M. Buhari as a quick, spicy
appetizer for soldiers and locals, with the 65 likely referring to the year or its menu number, becoming a
famous deep-fried chicken dish marinated in yogurt, ginger, garlic, and spices, garnished with curry leaves.
While theories suggest it meant 65 pieces, ingredients, or 65-day-old chicken, the most accepted story points to
its creation at Hotel Buhari in the year 1965, possibly as item #65 on the menu.

title: Pakhala Bhaat | Origin
lines: Pakhala Bhaat originated in the Eastern Indian subcontinent, deeply rooted in Odisha as a staple, but also popular
as Panta Bhat (Bengal), Poita Bhat (Assam), etc., evolving from necessity as a cooling, fermenting of cooked rice
in water (torani) to preserve it and beat the heat, mentioned in 17th-century texts and even linked to ancient
practices, becoming an integral part of Odia culture celebrated on March 20th (Pakhala Dibas).

title: Kashmiri Wazwan
lines: Kashmiri Lal Paneer (also called Laal Chaman) is unique for its vibrant red color and distinctive aromatic,
flavor profile that comes from specific regional spices, particularly fennel powder and dried ginger powder,
rather than onions, garlic, or excessive tomatoes as found in other Indian curries.
The authentic, traditional Kashmiri Pandit version (known as Waze Chaman) often omits onions,
garlic, and even tomatoes or yogurt, a stark difference from most other North Indian paneer dishes.
The gravy's body and thickness come from water and the spices themselves, sometimes supplemented with crushed fried onions or tomato puree in modern adaptations.
The paneer is typically shallow-fried until golden brown, then immediately transferred to a bowl
of warm water to keep it exceptionally soft and succulent while removing excess oil.

title: Chilli | Origin
lines: Chili peppers originated in the Americas, with the earliest evidence of cultivation in Mexico and
South America dating back thousands of years, used by civilizations like the Aztecs and Mayans for
food and medicine. Christopher Columbus encountered them in the 15th century, and European explorers
spread them globally via the Columbian Exchange, with Portuguese traders introducing them to India and
Asia by the 16th century, rapidly integrating them into cuisines worldwide.

title: Fenu Greek | Origin
lines: Originates from the Mediterranean region, Western Asia, and Southern Europe, though its exact center
of origin is debated, with some evidence pointing to the Near East where it was cultivated as far back
as 4000 BC. It's now a globally cultivated crop, vital in Indian, Middle Eastern, and North African
and traditional medicines, known for its maple-like scent and dual use as a spice and vegetable.

title: Potato | Origin
lines: The potato originated in the Andes Mountains of South America, specifically the region
of modern-day Peru and Bolivia, where it was first domesticated by indigenous peoples
like the Inca around 8,000 to 10,000 years ago. From its Andean roots, it spread globally
after Spanish explorers introduced it to Europe in the 16th century,
becoming a crucial global food crop.
